planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained, fertile to average, deep loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; Lawson cypress requires adequate moisture and good drainage. Highly susceptible to Phytophthora lateralis root rot — do not plant in poorly drained soils or where water stands. A critical threat to native populations in the Pacific Northwest.

directions: Plant container-grown trees in spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained, fertile to average, deep loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; Lawson cypress (Port Orford cedar) is one of the most important timber and ornamental conifers of the Pacific Coast — a massive forest tree native to a very restricted range in extreme southwestern Oregon and northwestern California, where it grows in the moist, foggy coast range forests and along mountain stream banks. The wood is extraordinarily lightweight, strong, aromatic, and durable — highly prized for boat-building, archery arrows, and as a Japanese export for shrine construction and coffins. In cultivation (especially in the UK and Europe where hundreds of cultivars have been selected), it is among the most diverse ornamental conifers, with columnar, pyramidal, pendulous, dwarf, and variously colored forms available. Severely threatened in its native range by the introduced water mold Phytophthora lateralis — a catastrophic root disease spread via contaminated water and soil. Widely grown in European gardens and as a hedging plant, where it thrives in cool, moist conditions.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, moist, fertile loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; Taiwan cypress grows naturally in mountain cloud forests of Taiwan at 3,000–9,000 feet elevation, requiring cool, humid conditions with excellent drainage. Rare in cultivation outside of botanical gardens and conifer specialist collections.

directions: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, moist, fertile loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; Taiwan cypress is one of the most massive and ancient conifers in Asia — the ancient forests of Taiwan contain trees that are the oldest and largest living organisms in East Asia, with some trees estimated at 2,000–3,000 years old and measuring over 60 feet in trunk circumference. The ancient tree 'Alishan Giant' and other Taiwan cypress giants in the Alishan Forest Recreation Area are Taiwan's most celebrated natural treasures. The reddish, furrowed bark and spreading, cathedral crown of these ancient specimens are extraordinary. In cultivation outside Taiwan it is rarely grown and poorly known compared to its relatives Chamaecyparis lawsoniana and C. obtusa. The wood has historically been an important construction timber in Taiwan and Japan. Grown primarily in botanical gardens and specialist collections in mild, humid climates — zones 7–9 with adequate rainfall.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moist to average, fertile to average loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; Korean arborvitae grows naturally on cool, rocky mountain slopes of Korea and is more cold-hardy and compact than Western red cedar (Thuja plicata), often growing as a low, spreading shrub rather than a tree at high elevation.

directions: Plant container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moist to average, fertile to average loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; Korean arborvitae is a distinctive and rarely grown species of arborvitae from the mountains of Korea — quite different in appearance from the familiar American arborvitae (Thuja occidentalis) or western red cedar (T. plicata). The foliage has striking silver-white stomatal marks on the undersides that create a beautiful bicolor effect of dark green above and silvery-white below when the branch tips are lifted or blown by wind. Naturally a variable species from low mounding shrubs at high elevations to narrow upright small trees at lower elevations. Aromatic foliage with a pleasant cedar scent when crushed. Rarely available from nurseries outside of specialist conifer growers, but increasingly of interest to gardeners seeking unusual conifers for their ornamental qualities. More compact and cold-hardy than giant arborvitae, making it appropriate for smaller gardens in colder climates.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic, moist, fertile loamy soil in partial shade; Japanese arborvitae grows naturally in humid mountain forests of Japan and requires adequate moisture and humidity — does not tolerate hot, dry, exposed conditions. Thrives in cool, maritime Pacific Northwest climates.

directions: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic, moist, fertile loamy soil in partial shade; Japanese arborvitae (hiba arborvitae) is the sole species of its genus — a large, impressive conifer from the humid mountain forests of Japan, closely related to Thuja but with distinctly larger, more robust, glossy-green scale leaves and a bold, dramatic texture compared to the finer-textured arborvitaes. The undersides of the branchlets show highly distinctive large, butterfly-shaped white stomatal markings on a dark green background — one of the most ornamentally striking foliage patterns of any conifer. The wood (hiba wood, Japanese: hiba) is exceptionally durable, aromatic, and rot-resistant — valued in Japan for temple construction, shrine buildings, and traditional Japanese architecture. The oils extracted from hiba wood have antifungal and antibacterial properties. In cultivation, it thrives in the cool, moist Pacific Northwest climate of the US and in the UK, where it is more commonly grown than in most of North America. An outstanding specimen conifer for large gardens in cool, humid climates.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, strongly acidic, cool, humus-rich, rocky soil in partial shade; Carolina hemlock grows naturally on rocky, exposed ridges and cliffs in the southern Appalachians — more tolerant of dry, rocky, exposed conditions than eastern hemlock but still requiring cool, humid, acidic conditions.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, strongly acidic, cool, humus-rich, rocky soil in partial shade; Carolina hemlock is a rare native conifer with a highly restricted range — endemic to the rocky ridges, cliff faces, and exposed rocky outcrops of the Blue Ridge Mountains in Virginia, the Carolinas, Georgia, and Tennessee, where it grows on sites too steep, rocky, and exposed for eastern hemlock. Distinguished from eastern hemlock (Tsuga canadensis) by its more open, irregular, rugged appearance, needles that radiate in all directions around the branchlet (rather than lying flat in two rows), and slightly larger cones. The narrow, rocky, clifftop habitat makes it more tolerant of drier, more exposed conditions than eastern hemlock. Like eastern hemlock, it is severely threatened by hemlock woolly adelgid (Adelges tsugae) — a devastating introduced pest responsible for the death of hemlocks across the eastern US. Valuable ornamental specimen tree for its bold texture and rugged appearance, preferred by some gardeners over eastern hemlock for its more irregular, natural form.
